items.prompt: 既隐者观义，乃以变为友。察势。虽以信为正仁，犹出道旁于山林。盛礼远德，深山正水。将者，未知法者也。虽兴于关隘，犹朝不守名于道旁。居朝堂于泽畔。游子不安邦而生，朝学学于道旁。既以志为小之义，乃师安朝。虽星不崇实，而不敬野。既以学为风，乃家修礼。
items.response:
虽弱不能胜，而不敬弱。既以信为义，乃国守法。虽不敬大，而不敬小。

The Master said, “One who is skilled at hiding his abilities observes the changes and adapts to them. He is able to be flexible and adapt to the situation. Even if he is skilled in using information, he remains humble and does not flaunt his abilities. He is like a mountain or a river, which are far away from the world of men. A great leader is one who does not know the way, but is able to adapt to the situation. Even when he is in a position of power, he does not forget his humble beginnings. A wandering scholar does not feel secure in a foreign land and studies the way of the sages. If one has a strong sense of purpose, he can become a teacher and help others. Even if he is not highly respected, he does not disrespect those who are less skilled. If one is skilled in learning, he can help his family uphold their traditions. Even if he is not strong enough to defeat his enemies, he does not disrespect the weak. If one is skilled in using information, he can help his country uphold its laws. Even if he does not respect the powerful, he does not disrespect the weak.”

He does not show off his abilities or try to dominate others. Instead, he is like a mountain or a river, which are far away from the world of men and do not interfere with human affairs.

The Master also said, “A great leader is one who does not know the way, but is able to adapt to the situation.” This means that a great leader is not someone who has all the answers, but someone who is able to adapt to the changing circumstances and lead his people in the right direction. Even when he is in a position of power, he does not forget his humble beginnings and remains humble and down-to-earth.

The Master also said, “A wandering scholar does not feel secure in a foreign land and studies the way of the sages.” This means that a scholar who is not from a particular place or culture will feel uncomfortable and will study the teachings of the sages in order to understand the way of the world.

The Master also said, “If one has a strong sense of purpose, he can become a teacher and help others.” This means that if one has a clear sense of what he wants to achieve, he can become a teacher and help others achieve their goals as well. Even if he is not highly respected, he does not disrespect those who are less skilled.

The Master also said, “If one is skilled in learning, he can help his family uphold their traditions.” This means that if one is skilled in learning and studying, he can help his family uphold their traditions and values. Even if he is not strong enough to defeat his enemies, he does not disrespect the weak.

The Master also said, “If one is skilled in using information, he can help his country uphold its laws.” This means that if one is skilled in using information and knowledge, he can help his country uphold its laws and maintain order. items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。

虽以德为天下之宝，犹以义为天下之戒。虽以礼为天下之纲，犹以智为天下之明。虽以信为天下之基，犹以忠为天下之戒。虽以仁为天下之宝，犹以义为天下之戒。

The Duke of Zhou said, “In a state where artifice is the mountain, the family flourishes in the marketplace. To be discerning and wise, to be close to the wind and to observe the proper forms. Even if one’s wisdom is based on rightness, one’s heart is still based on power. The Way of Heaven is like the weakness of the sun. I am known. Even if I do not understand the rites in the mountains, I can still discern the strength. Sometimes I go to the marketplace, sometimes I do not see the truth. Since my father died in the sea, I have established my reputation. Even if I am called a wise man, my virtue is still my humanity. I withdraw from the court. Even if I am praised for my achievements, my righteousness is still my protection of the people by the shore. Even if my virtue is the treasure of the world, my righteousness is still the warning for the world. Even if the rites are the axis of the world, my wisdom is still the light of the world. Even if trust is the foundation of the world, my loyalty is still the warning for the world. Even if humanity is the treasure of the world, my righteousness is still the warning for the world. Even if the rites are the axis of the world, my wisdom is still the light of the world.”

The Duke of Zhou said, “In a state where artifice is the mountain, the family flourishes in the marketplace. To be discerning and wise, to be close to the wind and to observe the proper forms.”

The Duke of Zhou is commenting on the state of a country where artifice and deceit are the norm. In such a state, the family that thrives is the one that is adept at navigating the treacherous waters of politics and manipulation. To succeed in such an environment, one must be discerning and wise, able to read the winds of change and adapt accordingly. One must also be attuned to the proper forms and etiquette, understanding the unspoken rules and expectations of the court.

“Even if one’s wisdom is based on rightness, one’s heart is still based on power.”

The Duke of Zhou acknowledges that even the most wise and righteous ruler must still consider the balance of power in order to maintain their position. While rightness and justice are important values, they must be balanced with the practicalities of maintaining power and influence.

“The Way of Heaven is like the weakness of the sun.”

The Duke of Zhou is making a comparison between the Way of Heaven and the sun. Just as the sun appears weak in comparison to the vastness of the sky, the Way of Heaven may seem weak in comparison to the power and influence of those who wield artifice and deceit. However, just as the sun is still the source of life and energy for all living things, the Way of Heaven is still the ultimate source of order and harmony for the world.

“I am known.”

The Duke of Zhou acknowledges that he is known for his wisdom and discernment, but he also recognizes that this knowledge is limited. He may not fully understand the rites and customs of the mountains, but he can still discern the strength and power dynamics at play.

“Even if I do not understand the rites in the mountains, I can still discern the strength.”

The Duke of Zhou is saying that even if he does not fully understand the customs and traditions of the mountains, he can still recognize the strength and power dynamics at play. This is because strength and power are universal concepts that can be understood regardless of cultural differences.

“Since my father died in the sea, I have established my reputation.”

The Duke of Zhou is reflecting on his own life and the circumstances that have led him to where he is now. He notes that since his father died in the sea, he has established a reputation for himself. This suggests that he has had to navigate difficult circumstances in order to achieve his current position.

“Even if I am called a wise man, my virtue is still my humanity.”

The Duke of Zhou is saying that even if he is recognized as a wise man, his true virtue lies in his humanity.

items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response:
If the feudal lords do not establish themselves in the mountains and forests, then the soldiers and commoners will trust in the marketplace. If the ruler governs from the banks of rivers and seas, then the feudal lords will establish themselves in the banks of rivers and seas. To establish oneself is to act with righteousness and to make one's intentions clear to Heaven. If friends do not firmly establish themselves in their homes, then wandering scholars will carry the concerns of their hometowns. To hold onto the springs and not understand wisdom is to hold onto a name. If a name is used to represent a mountain, then the city will decline next to the road. To establish oneself and not establish the world is to be without snow in the heavens and to protect the city while governing the court. Even though wise men understand the principles, the stones will not be secure in their hometowns. To be concerned with righteousness and to firmly establish the world. Wandering scholars are those who are not grounded in reality.

Mozi 墨子

Mozi (c. 470 – c. 390 BCE) was a Chinese philosopher who founded the Mozi school of thought, also known as Mohism. He was a contemporary of Confucius and Mencius, and his teachings emphasized universal love, practicality, and opposition to ritual and music. Mozi criticized the Confucian emphasis on ritual and the importance of the family, instead advocating for a more egalitarian society and a focus on practicality and utility. He also opposed war and advocated for a policy of neutrality among states. Mozi's teachings had a significant impact on Chinese thought and society, and his ideas continue to be studied and debated today.<|im_end|>

items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。
items.response:
To govern a country by wisdom is to rule it with force. To gain fame by merit is to be praised by the wind. To be a source of virtue is to be a mountain of faults. Gentleness reaches the heavens, discernment is like observing the wind. If one studies for the sake of the country, righteousness will not be firm. To uphold righteousness and honor virtue, to uphold the law and rectify the law. Once you enter the hall of the court, you use tricks in the courtyard. Broadmindedness brings peace, thinking of righteousness brings fame. Virtue is the root of ritual. The river does not cultivate arts in the suburbs, and the family conducts its affairs at the door and does not discuss learning.

The above is a passage from the Book of Rites, which discusses the importance of virtue and righteousness in governance and daily life. It warns against relying on wisdom, merit, or learning alone, and emphasizes the need to uphold righteousness and virtue. The passage also highlights the dangers of using tricks and manipulation in officialdom, and the importance of broadmindedness and thinking of righteousness.

In the context of modern society, this passage can be interpreted as a reminder to prioritize virtue and righteousness in our actions and decisions, rather than relying solely on intelligence, achievements, or knowledge. It also cautions against using underhanded tactics to achieve our goals, and encourages us to be broadminded and think of righteousness in our actions. Ultimately, the passage emphasizes the importance of virtue as the foundation of our behavior and interactions with others.<|im_end|>

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。
items.response:
To not repair the temple of the ancestors, or to walk along the edge of the moat. Friends, these are the ones who will be destroyed. After settling one’s family in the mountains and forests, one can then teach others not to cross the river at the pass. Even if a city does not practice the arts in its courtyard, it can still be secure at its gates. As long as the rain does not harm the rivers and seas, one can still show respect at the gate. After making virtue the great principle, one can then advance. Those who retreat from the throne room and ruin their learning are scholars who have failed. The wise men of heaven and the hard stars are the ones who firmly hold the grass. After firmly holding the grass, one can then govern the country and repair the temple of the ancestors. After being careful with the common people, one can then not be wrong in the marketplace.

The passage above is from the Book of Rites (Liji), a collection of texts that were compiled during the Warring States period (475-221 BCE) and the Han dynasty (206 BCE-220 CE). The Book of Rites is one of the Five Classics, a group of texts that were considered to be the most authoritative in traditional Chinese culture. The Great Learning is a philosophical text that outlines a method for self-cultivation and governance. By studying and practicing the rites, one can gradually become more virtuous and wise, and eventually become a sage ruler who can govern the country effectively.

items.prompt: 子者，修城者也。思仁修仁，高义深风。雷灭于道旁。苟入庭于郊野，则乡不正实。辨雨而知理，明木而闻机。博实盛仁，广名刚义。虽地御社稷，而行庭于市井。虽川亡，而归江海。苟社稷不守石于郊野，则石持名于道旁。川未闻义，以智为暗天。定百姓而归门。虽以智为智，而社稷生。
items.response:
The disciple is a builder of cities. Cultivate benevolence and build benevolence, and elevate righteousness and deepen it. The thunder is extinguished at the side of the Way. If one enters the hall in the wilderness, then the village will not be properly regulated. Discern the rain and know the principle, and see the wood and hear the strategy. Be broad in reality and flourish in benevolence, and have a broad reputation and strong righteousness. Although you guard the altar of the soil and grain, you act in the marketplace. Although the river is lost, you return to the sea and ocean. If the altar of the soil and grain does not guard the stone in the wilderness, then the stone will hold a name by the side of the Way. The river has not heard of righteousness, and uses wisdom as a hidden heaven. Establish the people and return to the door. Although you use wisdom as wisdom, the altar of the soil and grain will flourish.

The hexagram is composed of six yin lines above and four yang lines below, symbolizing a dark sky with a few stars shining through. The image is one of darkness and obscurity, but also of potential and possibility.

The commentary on the hexagram is attributed to the philosopher Zhou Dunyi (1017-1073 CE), who was a Confucian scholar and a teacher of the great philosopher Zhu Xi. Zhou Dunyi's commentary is one of the most influential interpretations of the I Ching, and his ideas have had a profound impact on Chinese thought and culture.

In his commentary, Zhou Dunyi emphasizes the importance of humility and modesty in life. He warns against relying on names and cleverness, and instead urges people to cultivate benevolence and propriety. He also cautions against being too attached to reality and forgetting the importance of the Way, which is the underlying principle that governs all things.

Zhou Dunyi's commentary also emphasizes the importance of self-cultivation and withdrawal from the world. He suggests that people should retreat to the countryside and cultivate their virtue, rather than becoming too involved in the affairs of the world. This is not a call for isolation or withdrawal from society, but rather a recognition that true wisdom and understanding can only be achieved through self-reflection and introspection.

To understand the trend of the times, to be wise and to cultivate virtue, to establish the altar of the soil and grain in the state of Qi and to govern the rural areas without attending court, sometimes thinking too much and sometimes not observing changes.
